local_names.romanized_local: Mutta Bonda / Muttai Bonda / Kodiguddu Bonda

region_of_origin: South India (Tamil Nadu, Kerala, Karnataka, Andhra Pradesh), India

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn term for Egg Bonda exists; mapped to broader class 'deep-fried food product' (FOODON_03315595) as the closest defensible anchor. Egg Bonda is a deep-fried, batter-coated hard-boiled egg snack.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FJP used as the closest defensible broader category for egg-based deep-fried preparations. No specific Egg Bonda entry exists in FoodEx2.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for Egg Bonda or equivalent South Indian deep-fried egg snack.

allergens: gluten (gram flour / besan may contain traces)

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of Egg Bonda is one piece, consisting of one whole hard-boiled egg coated in spiced gram flour batter and deep-fried, weighing approximately 80–100 g.

nutrient_source.title: Nutritive Value of Indian Foods
nutrient_source.organization: ICMR – National Institute of Nutrition (NIN), Hyderabad
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single Egg Bonda entry exists in ICMR-NIN tables. Values are adapted from ICMR-NIN data for hard-boiled egg and besan-based deep-fried snacks (e.g., pakora/bonda), combined with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for gram flour and rice flour components. Macros and micronutrients are weighted estimates reflecting approximately 55% egg and 45% batter (gram flour, rice flour, spices, absorbed oil) by cooked weight. USDA FoodData Central (https://fdc.nal.usda.gov/) used as secondary cross-reference for gram flour and egg nutrient profiles.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; University of Sydney GI Database — chickpea flour / besan-based fried snack analogues
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for Egg Bonda exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 35 is an analogous estimate based on: (1) chickpea flour (besan) pakora / gram flour fritter entries in the University of Sydney GI Database (GI range 28–40 for besan-based fried snacks); (2) the moderating effect of egg protein and dietary fat from deep-frying on glycemic response; (3) low net carbohydrate content (~12 g/100 g). Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI (35) × available carbohydrate per serving (90 g × 0.122 = 10.98 g) / 100 = 3.84, rounded to 3.8. This is classified as low GL (<10).

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (320 mg/100 g) and deep-fried preparation
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit Egg Bonda consumption due to its high sodium content (320 mg/100 g) and calorie-dense deep-fried preparation; if consumed, restrict to one piece occasionally and avoid adding salt-based condiments such as ketchup.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (estimated 35) but high fat and calorie density from deep-frying
type2_diabetes.guidance: Egg Bonda has a low estimated glycemic index (~35) and low glycemic load per serving (~3.8), making it less likely to cause acute blood glucose spikes; however, the high fat content (16.5 g/100 g) and calorie density (240 kcal/100 g) from deep-frying require portion control to avoid excess caloric intake in individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High total fat (16.5 g/100 g), saturated fat (~3.2 g/100 g), cholesterol (~185 mg/100 g), and sodium (320 mg/100 g) from deep-frying and egg yolk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Egg Bonda is not recommended as a regular food for individuals with cardiovascular disease due to its combination of high total fat, saturated fat, dietary cholesterol from egg yolk, and sodium; occasional consumption of one piece may be acceptable within a heart-healthy dietary pattern that is otherwise low in saturated fat and sodium.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Pro-inflammatory potential of deep-fried oils; egg provides anti-inflammatory micronutrients
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Eggs provide high-quality protein, selenium, and vitamin D which may support immune function; however, the deep-frying process can generate pro-inflammatory oxidised lipids, so individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should monitor their individual response and limit frequency of consumption.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Egg Bonda (also known as Egg Bajji or Mutta Bonda) is a popular South Indian street food and tea-time snack originating from the states of Tamil Nadu, Kerala, Karnataka, and Andhra Pradesh. It is prepared by coating whole or halved hard-boiled eggs in a thick, spiced batter made primarily from gram flour (besan) and rice flour, seasoned with red chili powder, turmeric, carom seeds (ajwain), asafoetida (hing), and salt, then deep-frying in hot vegetable oil until the outer crust is crispy and golden brown while the egg inside remains soft and fully cooked. The texture contrast between the crunchy, spiced batter exterior and the tender egg interior is a defining characteristic of this snack. Egg Bonda is commonly served with coconut chutney, green chutney, or spicy tomato ketchup and is a staple at South Indian tea stalls, street food carts, and home kitchens. Nutritionally, per 100 g it provides approximately 240 kcal, 11.5 g protein, 16.5 g fat, and 12.2 g carbohydrate, with meaningful amounts of vitamin B12 (0.8 mcg), selenium (18 mcg), vitamin A (95 mcg RAE), riboflavin (0.25 mg), and dietary fibre (2.5 g) from the gram flour batter. The sodium content is relatively high at 320 mg/100 g due to added salt and spices. Clinically, the estimated glycemic index is low (~35) due to the protein and fat content moderating glucose absorption, but the high calorie and fat density from deep-frying make portion control important for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease. It is suitable for vegetarians and is generally halal-compliant.
common_user_queries: Is Egg Bonda good for diabetics?
South Indian egg snack deep fried
Can I eat Egg Bonda if I have high blood pressure?

confidence_reason: No single exact Egg Bonda entry exists in ICMR-NIN published tables or USDA FoodData Central. Nutrient values are adapted estimates derived from ICMR-NIN data for hard-boiled eggs and besan-based fried snacks, cross-referenced with USDA SR Legacy data for gram flour and rice flour. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database entries for besan-based fried snack analogues. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are adapted estimates: no single published ICMR-NIN or USDA entry for Egg Bonda exists. Values reflect a weighted composition of approximately 55% hard-boiled egg and 45% gram flour / rice flour batter with absorbed frying oil, consistent with typical preparation ratios documented in ICMR-NIN and USDA SR Legacy component data. 2. water_g set to -1: moisture content for this composite deep-fried preparation is not directly reported in ICMR-NIN (Nutritive Value of Indian Foods) or USDA FoodData Central for this specific dish; accurate water content would require laboratory proximate analysis of the finished product. No approved source consulted reports a water_g value for Egg Bonda or a sufficiently close analogue. 3. saturated_fat_g (3.2 g), monounsaturated_fat_g (6.8 g), polyunsaturated_fat_g (4.5 g), trans_fat_g (0.1 g), and cholesterol_mg (185 mg) are estimated from USDA SR Legacy data for hard-boiled egg (FDC ID 173424) and gram flour (FDC ID 169725) in proportion to dish composition; these are not directly measured values. 4. GI of 35 is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database besan-based fried snack entries; no direct GI measurement for Egg Bonda has been published. 5. Portions in the input JSON contained three identical duplicate entries (all '1 serving 100g'); consolidated to a single representative serving of 90 g (one piece) for clinical relevance. 6. This dish is South Indian in origin; multilingual naming rules for Singaporean dishes do not apply. Tamil alias is provided as Tamil is one of the primary regional languages. Kannada, Malayalam, and Telugu aliases are included for completeness given the dish's multi-state South Indian origin. 7. FoodOn URI FOODON_03315595 and FoodEx2 code A0FJP are used as broader-class anchors; no exact ontology codes for Egg Bonda exist in published FoodOn or FoodEx2 releases as of 2024.

Iteration 3: Fixed G3 failure — added explicit documentation in notes field for water_g = -1, stating that moisture content for this composite deep-fried preparation is not reported in ICMR-NIN or USDA FoodData Central for this specific dish and that no approved source consulted reports a water_g value for Egg Bonda or a sufficiently close analogue.
